text: A eight-year-old man was admitted in emergency for a four-hour onset of acute abdominal pain. Previous medical history was consistent with six months non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs used for chronic abdominal pain. Physical examination disclosed acute peritoneitis with diffuse abdominal guarding and rebound turdiness. Hemodynamic status was normal. with slight tachycardia. Emergent CT scan showed three air bubbles next to a jejunal tract.

The laparoscopic exploration of the abdomen reveals localized inframesocolic acute peritonitis with visible small bowel content and fibrin deposits. The surgeon identifies a covert perforation in a solitary diverticulum located in the proximal jejunum during the complete small bowel examination.

The surgeon suspends the small bowel to the abdominal wall using percutaneous sutures. The involved jejunal tract is transected with an endo-GIA linear stapler, while the mesentery is divided using a ligature Maryland, two centimeters away from the bowel wall.

The surgeon aligns the two small bowel segments side by side in an isoperistaltic orientation, suspending them to the abdominal wall. A side-to-side jejuno-jejunal intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using an endo-GIA linear stapler inserted through two small enterotomies. The enterotomies are then closed with a barbed running suture.

The surgeon places a reinforcing non-absorbable monofilament suture at the lower edge, following the closure of the mesenteric defect. This is preceded by the application of a first extra-mucosal layer and a second seromuscular layer.

The surgeon performs lavage of the abdominal cavity using two liters of saline solution, leaving a twenty-four French silicone drain in place. The resected jejunal tract is extracted into an endo bag and visually inspected.
Post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged at home on the sixth post-operative day.
